replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz Viano 3.0 / 3.5 CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the W639 Viano OM642 generation — a single engine family with different power outputs. A replacement engine of type 642.990 costs on average £4,290 in good condition. A new or reconditioned engine can cost up to £5,747. All pricing is indicative; actual costs vary by mileage, condition and workshop location.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Viano 3.0/3.5 CDI engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The V6 is tightly packaged in the Viano's engine bay.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz Viano 3.0 / 3.5 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential repair — can cause engine failure if ignored
Essential — can cause belt failure and overheating

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes-Benz Viano 3.0 / 3.5 CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2008 facelift model — electrical and suspension improvements
* Documented oil cooler seal history — replacement with updated seals
* Full service history with regular oil changes and Mercedes-approved oil
* EGR system recently cleaned or replaced
* No cold-start rattle — timing chain health
avoidIf: * Any evidence of oil/coolant contamination — oil cooler seal failure risk
* Hesitation between 1700–1900 rpm — EGR valve clogging
* Pre-2008 model with electrical issues — known gremlins
* Poor service history — the OM642 is unforgiving of neglect
* Any cold-start rattle — timing chain or water pump issues

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Viano 3.0/3.5 CDI offers refined V6 diesel power in a spacious MPV package. The OM642 is a smooth, torquey engine but has well-documented weaknesses — oil cooler seals in the V of the engine are the defining issue, while EGR clogging, water pump seizures, and timing chain wear are also common. A post-2008 facelift car with full service history and documented work on the key weak points is the buy. Buy on history, maintain the oil cooler and EGR systems, and the Viano will serve you well.

cta.label: → Compare Mercedes-Benz Viano 3.0/3.5 CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz Viano 3.0 / 3.5 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup (timing chain or water pump warning)
* Service History — Check the year to identify which generation — pre-2008 electrical issues
* Oil / Coolant Check — Check expansion tank for mayonnaise (oil cooler seal failure)
* Hesitation Test — Test drive; any bucking between 1700–1900 rpm? (EGR clogging)
* Water Pump — Any belt squeal or coolant loss? (water pump seizure risk)
* Injector Area — Check for black carbon residue around injectors (injector seal leak)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? EGR and injector codes are common
* Rust Check — Viano chassis and sills are prone to rust

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz Viano 3.0 / 3.5 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned Mercedes-Benz Viano 3.0/3.5 CDI engine (OM642) typically costs £3,500–£6,000 supply only. A replacement engine of type 642.990 costs on average £4,290 in good condition; a new or reconditioned engine can cost up to £5,747. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800. An oil cooler seal replacement costs £800–£1,500. Check the Era Map above for your engine code.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz Viano 3.0 / 3.5 CDI engine reliable?
items.answer: The OM642 V6 diesel is a refined, smooth engine but has well-documented weaknesses. Oil cooler seals in the V of the engine are the defining issue — they can leak oil into the coolant or externally. EGR clogging, water pump seizures, and timing chain wear are also common. A post-2008 facelift car with full service history and documented work on the key weak points is the best bet.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz Viano 3.0 / 3.5 CDI?
items.answer: Oil cooler seal leak (oil/coolant contamination, £800–£1,500), EGR valve clogging (hesitation between 1700–1900 rpm, £500–£1,200), water pump seizure (belt failure, overheating, £400–£800), timing chain wear (cold-start rattle, £1,500–£3,500), injector failure and "black death" (£300–£800 per injector), and turbo actuator failure (£500–£1,500). The 3.5 CDI is essentially the same engine with a higher tune.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz Viano 3.0 / 3.5 CDI engine, or should I scrap the van?
items.answer: Viano values range from £4,000–£15,000 depending on generation, condition and mileage. A £3,500–£6,000 reconditioned engine represents 25–80% of vehicle value — our 70% Mercedes-Benz scrap threshold means careful evaluation is required. For a well-maintained, low-mileage example, replacement is often justified. For an ageing van with significant rust or other structural issues, assess scrap value first.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz Viano 3.0 / 3.5 CDI engine code?
items.answer: The Viano 3.0 and 3.5 CDI both use the OM642 engine code — Mercedes' 3.0-litre V6 diesel. The 3.0 CDI produces 204–224 bhp (204 PS / 150 kW), while the 3.5 CDI produces 231 bhp. The code is stamped on the engine block; your VIN and vehicle documentation will also confirm the specification.

items.question: Can I repair the oil cooler seal issue, or does it need replacement?
items.answer: Yes — the oil cooler seals can be replaced (£800–£1,500). This is the most common OM642 repair. The oil cooler is located in the V of the engine; replacing the seals requires removing the intake manifold. If you see oil in the coolant (mayonnaise in the expansion tank) or coolant in the oil, stop driving immediately and book a specialist inspection. Ignored seal failure can lead to bearing damage and engine failure.
